title: Prime Minister

jokes.text: Murray marks himself as 'present' without letting Bret and Jemaine say their own 'presents', then claims it's to save time.
jokes.setup: Murray is conducting a band meeting and calls roll, marking each member present himself.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine protests that this wouldn't have saved much time, exposing Murray's petty control-freakery as the real motive.
jokes.explanation: A tidy character joke that efficiently establishes Murray's bureaucratic absurdity — he performs efficiency theater in a way that is itself inefficient. Jemaine's deadpan deflation is perfectly calibrated. The joke works because it's so specific to Murray's personality type.

dialogue.speaker: Brett
dialogue.line: present.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: present.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Why didn't you let us say present?

jokes.text: Murray announces the prime minister of New Zealand is coming, and that he might even 'meet the president of America' — said as though America needs the qualifier.
jokes.setup: Murray announces the visit of the NZ prime minister, framing it as a major diplomatic event.
jokes.punchline: The grandiosity of 'we might even go to Washington and meet the president of America' undercuts itself — the qualifier 'of America' implies the audience might be confused about which president.
jokes.explanation: Understated Kiwi parochialism — 'the president of America' is a small but telling detail that signals this show's consistent joke about New Zealanders being slightly out of their depth on the world stage.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I'm trying to save time here, Jemaine.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That wouldn't have saved much time.

jokes.text: Bret asks 'Will the queen be there?' in response to the news of a small party with the president.
jokes.setup: Murray says it's 'not a big do, just a small party' about the presidential meeting.
jokes.punchline: Bret asks if the Queen will be there, an absurdly escalating non-sequitur that also reveals his total lack of political sophistication.
jokes.explanation: Perfectly economical absurdist character comedy. Murray has just described a small event and Bret immediately assumes the Queen might be there. It works because it's so earnest and so wrong simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Do you want to know why?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: All right, I'll tell you.

jokes.text: After Greg is told 'Dismissed,' Bret or Jemaine observes: 'Likes to lurk there, doesn't he?'
jokes.setup: Greg silently exits after being dismissed.
jokes.punchline: One of the guys deadpans a mild observation about Greg's lurking as if it's a notable personality trait, extending the absurdity.
jokes.explanation: A small callback that extends the lurking joke. Works as a mild runner but doesn't add much beyond agreeing with the previous beat.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Will the queen be at the president meeting?

jokes.text: Murray: 'I listened to some of their songs, and they're actually better than your songs.'
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to convince Bret and Jemaine to perform Simon & Garfunkel songs.
jokes.punchline: His justification is breathtakingly blunt — Simon & Garfunkel are literally better than them — delivered as though this is a helpful observation.
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional. The comedy manager is supposed to advocate for his act, but Murray with complete sincerity tells them Simon & Garfunkel are better. The economy is near-perfect — maximum brutality in minimum words. It's also perfectly in character: Murray's loyalty is always to the path of least resistance rather than to his clients. Comparable in craft to the '8.0 anchor' for similar economy and character specificity.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, you're there, greg, lurking. How long have you been lurking there?

jokes.text: Murray: 'Well, here's the thing — the actual lookalikes don't look anything alike, and they don't look like the guys they're supposed to look like, and they're busy. So I went in there and you know what? You look just like they do.'
jokes.setup: Murray is explaining how he got the band a gig from a lookalike agency despite them not looking like Simon & Garfunkel.
jokes.punchline: The logic is gloriously circular: they look like the lookalikes who don't look like Simon & Garfunkel, therefore they qualify.
jokes.explanation: This is a beautifully constructed piece of absurdist logic. The chain of non-resemblance — lookalikes who don't look like their subjects, and our heroes who look like those lookalikes — creates a comedy of cascading inadequacy. Murray delivers it with the confidence of someone who has solved a problem rather than compounded one. Strong craft score earned.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I got you both something.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Jemaine, this is for you.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Thanks, Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: And Bret, this is for you.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Cheers.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I thought you might like them. They're, uh, gifts.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: What are they?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Clothes. And a wig.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Why would you give us a wig?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I thought it might be fun. You know, for a costume party or something.

jokes.text: After the boys refuse, Murray says 'Okay. Well, I'll have to put that $50 back then.' Pause. '$50 each.'
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine flatly refuse to do the tribute act gig.
jokes.punchline: Murray drops the money figure as a last resort, then upgrades it from $50 total to $50 each — watching them reconsider in real time.
jokes.explanation: The beat structure is excellent: flat refusal, pause, '$50,' longer pause, '$50 each' — letting us watch their resolve melt in stages. The comedy is in the beats between the lines as much as the lines themselves.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: A wig? What does Bret need a...

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah. It's for the gig, okay?

jokes.text: Bret and Jemaine performing 'Scarborough Fair' as Simon & Garfunkel — one wearing an afro wig.
jokes.setup: The boys have agreed to do the Simon & Garfunkel tribute act for $50 each.
jokes.punchline: We see them on stage actually doing it — the visual of two tall New Zealanders in wigs performing folk songs is the payoff to the entire first-act setup.
jokes.explanation: The visual payoff for a well-constructed first act. Not a joke in itself but the realization of the comedic premise. Earns its score through context and performance rather than writing.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: 'I listened to some of their songs, and they're actually better than your songs'

jokes.text: Backstage: 'Look at that, man. They've got apples.'
jokes.setup: After performing, they're backstage at the lookalike event discovering the perks.
jokes.punchline: The most exciting thing about their gig is free apples — the complete deflation of rock star backstage expectations.
jokes.explanation: Four words that perfectly capture the band's humble circumstances and low expectations. The apple excitement against a backdrop of performing as tribute acts at a lookalike agency party is quietly devastating.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Well, technically it was a gig. We put your names down on the karaoke list.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: That's not a gig, Murray.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It was a paying gig.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We had to pay to get in.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, but you got to sing.

jokes.text: The 'Elton John' impersonator tells Jemaine 'It's so good to see someone else up there just like me, doing my thing' — Jemaine points out he's doing Elton John's thing, not his own.
jokes.setup: The Elton John lookalike approaches with fellow-artist camaraderie.
jokes.punchline: Jemaine correctly notes he's doing Elton John's thing. The impersonator insists 'my thing is to do Elton John's thing' — a circular identity crisis played completely straight.
jokes.explanation: A tight philosophical loop about artistic identity that also exposes the Conchords' own situation — they're doing Simon & Garfunkel's thing while claiming their own artistic integrity. The impersonator's defense is perfectly circular and gets progressively more tangled the more he explains it.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You've been managing us for two years.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Yeah, I know, but that's the first time I've been to that karaoke bar.

jokes.text: The Elton impersonator greets the second Elton impersonator with 'Oh, hold me closer, tiny dancer. Hello.'
jokes.setup: The Elton impersonator spots his hated rival in the same outfit.
jokes.punchline: His greeting incorporates an Elton John lyric as if it's casual speech — staying in character even while confronting his enemy.
jokes.explanation: A compact and elegant joke — the Elton character is so deep in the persona that Elton John lyrics have become his vernacular. The deadpan delivery of 'Hello' after the lyric is the key comedic beat.

dialogue.line: Murray's explanation about lookalikes who don't look like anyone

jokes.text: The Bono lookalike is described as 'like Bono from U2, but real muscular.'
jokes.setup: The impersonator is pointing out other celebrity lookalikes at the event.
jokes.punchline: The qualifier 'but real muscular' implies the real Bono is not muscular — a tiny, specific, observational detail.
jokes.explanation: A small, functional joke. The visual of a hyper-muscular Bono impersonator is amusing but the joke does limited work.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: 'So we look like some simon & garfunkel lookalikes who don't look like simon & garfunkel?'

jokes.text: The woman who will become Jemaine's date says 'I like your height' and Jemaine responds 'I'm usually this height.'
jokes.setup: A woman at the event is complimenting Jemaine (as Art Garfunkel).
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's response to a compliment about his height is to confirm he is always this height — a perfectly literal, socially oblivious non-answer.
jokes.explanation: An instant classic of Flight of the Conchords deadpan. The pairing with 'I usually look better than this' (implying the wig is an improvement) makes these two lines a perfect comedy couplet. 'I'm usually this height' has zero wasted syllables and is a perfect articulation of Jemaine's earnest, literal social incompetence. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: $50 each.

jokes.text: Bret starts to say 'The prime minis—' and Murray frantically shushes him, pointing at the sleeping prime minister.
jokes.setup: The prime minister has apparently fallen asleep in the middle of the backstage area.
jokes.punchline: Bret almost inadvertently reveals his identity; Murray's panicked shushing reveals the prime minister is just unconscious in public.
jokes.explanation: A good visual setup for the prime minister's introduction. The jet lag as a recurring condition that renders him narcoleptic in public is a solid comedic premise.

dialogue.speaker: Elton
dialogue.line: I'm doing my thing. He's doing his thing. My thing is doing his thing, but it's my thing, not his thing.

jokes.text: The prime minister wakes up confused: 'What day is it, yesterday?' Murray: 'No, it's today, sir.'
jokes.setup: The prime minister wakes from jet-lag sleep, disoriented.
jokes.punchline: 'What day is it, yesterday?' is a grammatically confused question that encapsulates total temporal disorientation.
jokes.explanation: 'What day is it, yesterday?' is a perfectly absurd line that sounds almost reasonable when you're jet-lagged but is grammatically impossible. Murray's deadpan 'No, it's today, sir' takes it seriously, which is the correct response. Highly quotable.

dialogue.line: Elton's outrage at another Elton John impersonator doing the same outfit

jokes.text: Bret addresses the prime minister as 'your honor' and Jemaine calls him 'my lord' — wrong honorifics for a prime minister.
jokes.setup: The boys meet the prime minister of New Zealand.
jokes.punchline: They use the wrong honorifics — 'your honor' (a judge) and 'my lord' (British nobility) — revealing complete ignorance of protocol.
jokes.explanation: A concise character comedy beat. Both wrong titles are plausible stabs in the dark by people who have never met a head of government. 'My lord' escalates nicely from 'your honor.' The PM doesn't react, which is funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Elton impersonator
dialogue.line: There is a guy who is not doing his own thing. There's a guy who doesn't have a single original idea in his head. He's dressed... he's doing elton John's thing. He looks like you.

jokes.text: The prime minister's primary concern after flying from New Zealand: finding out what happens at the end of the movie 'Cars.'
jokes.setup: The prime minister of New Zealand has just arrived in New York for an important diplomatic mission.
jokes.punchline: His pressing issue is that his screen broke halfway through 'Cars' and he needs to know if the main car and girl car get together.
jokes.explanation: This is the standout joke of the first half. A dignitary's most pressing diplomatic matter is a Pixar romantic subplot. The specificity — 'specifically between the main car and the girl car' — makes it extraordinary. Murray routing the query through Greg ('the prime minister wants to know what happens at the end of the movie Cars...') as if it's official state business is perfect. The PM's satisfied 'Good. It was touch and go there for a while' is a perfect topper. High craft score fully earned.

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: This is parsley and this is sage.

dialogue.speaker: Host
dialogue.line: Hello, sage.

jokes.text: Murray explains that Bret can be the bodyguard by pretending he has a gun with his hand in his jacket. 'Don't pull that out, 'cause that's obviously not a gun. When you pull it out, just go: It's still in there.'
jokes.setup: They need a bodyguard for the PM.
jokes.punchline: Murray's bodyguard protocol is to mime having a gun and, if challenged, claim the (clearly absent) gun is 'still in there.'
jokes.explanation: 'It's still in there' as a security strategy is a genuinely great joke. The specificity of the instruction — preemptively accounting for the failure state of the disguise by providing a follow-up lie — shows Murray planning several steps ahead while remaining completely useless. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Date
dialogue.line: I usually look better than this.

dialogue.speaker: Date
dialogue.line: I like your height.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I'm usually this height.

jokes.text: The salesman explains the FBI-style earpieces: 'These have actually been worn by the FBI on several occasions.'
jokes.setup: Bret is being equipped with FBI-style earpieces for his bodyguard role.
jokes.punchline: 'On several occasions' is a deliberately vague and minimal endorsement — not 'regularly used by the FBI' but 'worn on several occasions,' which could mean almost nothing.
jokes.explanation: A small but precise joke. 'On several occasions' is beautifully non-committal — it's a non-endorsement dressed as an endorsement. The joke is in the qualifier.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: The prime minister. Yes, he's terribly jet-lagged. He thinks it's tomorrow. Don't wake him.

jokes.text: Murray's phone call to the White House: 'It is a country! Yes, it is!' after apparently being questioned on whether New Zealand exists.
jokes.setup: Murray is trying to call the White House to arrange the presidential meeting.
jokes.punchline: The White House staff apparently don't know (or pretend not to know) that New Zealand is a country, and Murray is forced to defend its existence.
jokes.explanation: A recurring theme of the show — New Zealand's invisibility on the world stage — executed with a specific, fresh scenario. Murray having to assert New Zealand's existence to White House staff is both a political commentary and a perfect character humiliation.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: Do you like bubble baths? Sorry, I'm getting ahead of myself.

jokes.text: Karen reveals the wig was the attraction: 'That was a big orange afro wig... you wouldn't mind going home and changing into what you were wearing the other night, would you? I was really attracted to that, and now this.'
jokes.setup: On the date, Karen asks what Jemaine did to his hair. He explains the afro was a wig.
jokes.punchline: She asks him to go home and change into the Art Garfunkel costume because she finds the real Jemaine a step down.
jokes.explanation: The reveal that Jemaine was more attractive in the wig than his natural state is a beautiful inversion of his earlier claim to 'usually look better.' The callback lands because it contradicts his self-image precisely. Karen's blunt 'I was really attracted to that, and now this' is very funny cringe.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: asking what day it is 'yesterday?'

jokes.text: Morning after the date: Bret takes a spoonful of cereal 'so it looks like we're having breakfast.'
jokes.setup: Morning scene; Jemaine has been away for three days.
jokes.punchline: Bret is performing the performance of normalcy — eating cereal so it 'looks like' breakfast — implying he's been staged for Jemaine's return.
jokes.explanation: A quietly wonderful joke. 'So it looks like we're having breakfast' — who is this performance for? Why is Bret staging the scene? The casual absurdism of this opening line sets a perfect tone. Highly economical.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Prime Minister's obsession with the ending of Cars movie

jokes.text: Bret asks 'Why are you wearing your Art Garfunkel costume?' — Jemaine has come home still in the wig after three days.
jokes.setup: Jemaine returns after three days away.
jokes.punchline: He's still in the Art Garfunkel costume, and Jemaine's explanation is simply 'She just prefers me to wear it.'
jokes.explanation: The reveal of the wig's continued deployment is the comedic payoff of the whole date arc. 'She just prefers me to wear it' is a perfect deadpan surrender.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: Good. It was touch and go there for a while. Thank you for that, Murray. That was well done.

jokes.text: Karen takes a photo of Jemaine in the Art Garfunkel wig: 'Just one more photo, okay? Just one more.' Then another. 'It's not weird.'
jokes.setup: Karen is in their apartment photographing Jemaine in the costume.
jokes.punchline: 'It's not weird' is said by someone about something that is extremely weird, with no apparent self-awareness.
jokes.explanation: The 'it's not weird' unprompted self-defense is a standard cringe beat. Works but isn't exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Don't pull that out, 'cause that's obviously not a gun. When you pull it out, just go 'it's still in there.'

jokes.text: Mel tells Jemaine 'Did she slap you around? Did she sleep around on you? Did she leave you?' and is progressively more confused when the answer to all is no.
jokes.setup: Mel sees Jemaine looking upset and assumes Karen has wronged him.
jokes.punchline: All of Mel's guesses involve conventional victimhood scenarios. The real answer is more unusual.
jokes.explanation: A functional setup for the 'garfunkeling' reveal. The rapid-fire wrong guesses are a standard comedy escalation device. Works as mechanics rather than as a great joke in itself.

dialogue.speaker: Bystander
dialogue.line: What language is he talking?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: English.

jokes.text: Karen opens the door in her apartment to find the real Art Garfunkel standing there. She announces inside: 'Art Garfunkel is at the door.'
jokes.setup: The episode has been building around a woman's Art Garfunkel obsession.
jokes.punchline: The actual Art Garfunkel arrives at her door — making the whole subplot suddenly make sense and simultaneously much weirder.
jokes.explanation: The structural payoff for the entire Jemaine/Karen subplot. The real Art Garfunkel's arrival recontextualizes everything — Karen wasn't just a strange fan of the music, she apparently has a real history with Art Garfunkel himself. 'Art Garfunkel is at the door' delivered to an apartment containing Jemaine in an Art Garfunkel wig is a perfectly constructed visual irony.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Dave, I'm going on a date with a woman tonight.

dialogue.speaker: Dave
dialogue.line: Gonna bang her?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: She didn't mention that. She mentioned dinner.

jokes.text: Murray introduces 'the 44th president of the United States' — and it's a man from a lookalike agency who 'also does Usher and sometimes Will Smith.'
jokes.setup: Murray announces he's 'pulled some strings' and gotten the president to attend the fondue party.
jokes.punchline: The 'president' is a lookalike who also does Usher and Will Smith — from the same agency as the Simon & Garfunkel lookalikes. Murray has 'solved' the diplomatic problem by hiring an impersonator.
jokes.explanation: The episode's structural climax — Murray's solution to the diplomatic crisis is to hire a presidential lookalike from the same agency where he booked Bret and Jemaine. Everything comes full circle: the lookalike-of-lookalikes joke, the 'what a blessing' logic, now applied to international diplomacy. The fake president's multi-character repertoire (Usher, Will Smith) establishes his credentials as a generalist lookalike. An excellent structural callback.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Murray's desperate call to White House insisting New Zealand is a country

jokes.text: The PM requests a photo 'like we're Charlie's Angels' — 'cause this one's for my kid's room. Really big Charlie's Angels fan.'
jokes.setup: The photo session continues.
jokes.punchline: The prime ministerial photo session ends with a Charlie's Angels pose for the kids' room — the escalation from diplomatic photos to action-hero poses is perfect.
jokes.explanation: The pivot from 'making it look like we've done a really important trade deal' to Charlie's Angels for the kids' room is an excellent escalation. Brian's earnest domestic reason ('really big Charlie's Angels fan') makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Prime Minister
dialogue.line: You know what that is, don't you? Glitch in the matrix. Fuckin' a.

tmdb_overview: Murray sets up a meeting for the New Zealand prime minister; Jemaine gets mixed up with an Art Garfunkel fan at the end of a tribute gig.
